Determine the beam support conditions (e.g., simply supported).
Identify the type and location of the applied load.
Determine the material properties (E) and section geometry (I).
Select the appropriate deflection formula.
Calculate the maximum deflection and compare with allowable limits.
Include self-weight as equivalent distributed load when span is long.
Compare computed δ to code serviceability limit for the application.
Identify the bearing type and basic dynamic load rating (C).
Calculate the equivalent dynamic load (P) based on radial and axial forces.
Determine the life exponent (p) based on bearing geometry.
Apply the formula to find life in millions of revolutions.
Convert revolutions to hours if rotational speed is known.
Convert combined radial and axial loads to equivalent P per ISO rules.
Convert L10 in millions of revolutions to hours using operating speed.
Select the chip breaker geometry based on the material (e.g., TF for general, TQ for stability).
Choose the feed method: Radial Infeed vs. Flank Infeed (3°–5°).
Determine the number of passes based on thread pitch (from tables).
Monitor chip flow direction using the asymmetric point logic.
Adjust cutting speed (Vc) based on tool wear patterns.
Set first threading pass depth above minimum chip breaker activation ap.
Apply high-pressure coolant for internal threads (SINR/CINR operations).
Identify the source unit (e.g., mm).
Identify the target unit (e.g., inch).
Apply the conversion factor.
Verify the magnitude of the result.
Round to the appropriate significant figures.
Document the unit system on the calculation worksheet header.
Convert temperature to kelvin when using gas laws or thermal expansion.
Identify all external forces acting on the body.
Draw a Free Body Diagram (FBD).
Resolve forces into X, Y, and Z components.
Apply equilibrium equations: ΣFx=0, ΣFy=0.
Sum moments about a point to find unknowns: ΣM=0.
Check that the number of unknowns equals independent equilibrium equations.
Verify results by substituting reactions back into a second moment center.
Determine bolt size (e.g., M10).
Find recommended preload (from standards or tables).
Estimate friction coefficient (lubricated vs dry).
Convert diameter to meters.
Apply the formula: T = K × F × d.
Verify against torque tables.
Select μ_thread and μ_head from lubrication condition tables.
Verify yield utilization and thread stripping for both internal and external threads.
Identify the motion required (Linear vs Rotational).
Select the power transmission element (Gear, Belt, Chain).
Choose the support element (Bearing, Shaft).
Specify the joining method (Bolt, Weld, Rivet).
Verify sizing using engineering standards.
Estimate duty cycle and environmental exposure before catalog lookup.
Verify interchangeability with supplier drawings and ISO designation.
Calculate the internal force at the point of interest.
Determine the cross-sectional area of the element.
Apply the stress formula: σ = F / A.
Calculate strain using Hooke's Law: ε = σ / E.
Verify that stress is below the material's yield strength.
Apply stress concentration factor Kt when geometry has discontinuities.
Compare computed stress to yield with an appropriate factor of safety.
Determine the initial stress state (σx, σy, τxy).
Plot the points (σx, τxy) and (σy, -τxy) on the stress axes.
Draw the circle passing through these two points.
Locate the center and calculate the radius (R).
Identify σ1 (max) and σ2 (min) as the intersections with the horizontal axis.
Plot σx, σy, τxy on Mohr circle to read σ1, σ2, and τ_max.
Compare τ_max to yield/2 for ductile materials per Tresca criterion.
Determine the required output torque (T).
Determine the operational speed (n) in RPM.
Apply the formula to find the power in kW.
Account for transmission efficiency if necessary.
Select a motor with a power rating higher than the calculated requirement.
Identify peak vs continuous torque for duty classification.
Convert between kW and HP using 1 HP ≈ 0.746 kW when reading US catalogs.
Determine fluid properties (density, viscosity).
Calculate the Reynolds number to determine flow type (laminar vs turbulent).
Find the friction factor (f) using the Moody chart or equations.
Apply the Darcy-Weisbach equation.
Account for local losses (valves, elbows) if applicable.
Compute Reynolds number Re = ρVD/μ to select friction factor.
Sum minor losses from fittings using equivalent length or K factors.
Identify the primary thread system (Metrik vs. Inch).
Verify the profile angle (e.g., 60° for ISO Metrik).
Select the appropriate tolerance class (4h for tight, 6g for standard).
Calculate the major and minor diameters based on the pitch.
Choose the correct machining tool based on the profile.
Calculate stress area As from pitch and pitch diameter for strength checks.
Verify tap drill size against minor diameter before production run.
For Torsion: Calculate the polar moment of inertia (J).
Apply the torsion formula: τ = (T × r) / J.
For Buckling: Determine the column end conditions (Fixed, Pinned).
Identify the least moment of inertia (weak axis).
Calculate the critical buckling load (Pcr) using Euler's formula.
Compute polar moment J for solid and hollow shafts under torque.
Check slenderness ratio L/r against code limits for Euler validity.
Excessive deflection can cause structural instability or functional failure (e.g., doors not closing).
Allows engineers to optimize beam size and material to meet safety standards with minimal cost.
Prevents unexpected machine downtime by predicting replacement intervals.
Ensures bearings are correctly sized for the specific operating environment and load conditions.
Incorrect chip control leads to 'bird-nesting', which is the #1 cause of tool failure.
Modified flank feed (3°–5°) significantly improves tool life by distributing heat.
Incorrect unit conversion led to the loss of the Mars Climate Orbiter.
Manufacturing tolerances are often specified in microns (μm), requiring high precision.
Ensures structures and machines can withstand applied loads without moving or failing.
Allows for the calculation of reaction forces at supports.
Improper torque leads to bolt fatigue, joint separation, and uneven load distribution.
In industries like automotive and aerospace, torque accuracy is critical for safety and reliability.
Standardized elements allow for interchangeable parts and easier maintenance.
Knowing the right element for the job prevents over-engineering and reduces cost.
Prevents structural failure by ensuring material limits are never exceeded.
Allows for the selection of appropriate materials based on loading conditions.
Ductile materials typically fail due to maximum shear stress (τmax).
Brittle materials fail due to maximum principal stress (σ1).
Mohr's Circle allows designers to predict the angle of failure.
Prevents motor burnout due to undersizing.
Avoids excessive energy costs and initial investment due to oversizing.
Inadequate pump sizing leads to system failure or insufficient flow.
Excessive pressure drop results in high energy consumption and piping wear.
Profile mismatch (e.g., trying to fit a UN into an M hole) destroys threads immediately.
Tolerance grades (6g vs 4h) determine the 'fit' and assembly ease in mass production.
Prevents catastrophic collapse of long structural members.
Ensures power transmission shafts can handle rotational torque without failure.
Mixing units (e.g., using mm for I but m for L).
Using the wrong formula for the specific support or loading condition.
Neglecting the beam's own weight for very long spans.
Using static load ratings instead of dynamic load ratings in the formula.
Failing to account for axial loads when calculating the equivalent dynamic load.
Assuming constant speed and load when both vary in actual operation.
Using constant depth of cut for all passes (the first pass must be deeper).
Failing to use high-pressure coolant in internal threading (SINR/CINR).
Neglecting the minimum depth of cut (ap) required to trigger the chip breaker.
Using decimal points incorrectly in different locales (comma vs point).
Mixing mass (kg) and force (N) units in calculations.
Neglecting temperature unit shifts (Celsius to Kelvin).
Failing to include all forces in the Free Body Diagram.
Incorrectly identifying the direction (sign) of forces or moments.
Neglecting the weight of the object when it is significant.
Ignoring lubrication effects on friction coefficient.
Using generic torque tables blindly without considering specific application context.
Not accounting for friction variation between threads and under-head surfaces.
Choosing an element without considering the environment (Corrosion, Heat).
Neglecting the dynamic loads (Vibration) on static fasteners.
Using non-standard sizes that are hard to source.
Neglecting the sign of the stress (Tension vs Compression).
Using the wrong area for shear stress (it's the area parallel to the force).
Failing to account for stress concentrations at holes or fillets.
Confusing the 2θ angle in Mohr's Circle with the θ angle in the physical world.
Incorrectly plotting the sign of the shear stress.
Forgetting to check the 3D stress state (Triaxial stress).
Forgetting to include the efficiency of the gearbox or transmission.
Using HP units directly in a kW formula without conversion.
Failing to account for peak starting torque.
Ignoring the effect of pipe roughness on the friction factor.
Assuming laminar flow when it is actually turbulent.
Failing to convert units correctly (e.g., bar to Pascal).
Mixing up 60° (Metric) and 55° (Whitworth) threads, which look nearly identical.
Ignoring thread tolerances (6g/6H) in critical structural applications.
Using partial profile tools for full-profile requirements.
Using the strongest axis for buckling (it always fails on the weakest).
Neglecting the slenderness ratio (L/r) limits.
Ignoring combined loading (Torsion + Bending).
Always verify units twice; displacement units are often small and sensitive to power-of-ten errors.
Always apply safety factors and consider environmental conditions like temperature and lubrication quality.
Always use SI units for internal calculations and only convert to Imperial for final presentation if required.
Always choose a point with the most unknown forces when summing moments to simplify the math.
Always validate torque with preload-based calculations, not just tables.
Always design for disassembly unless the joint is intended to be permanent.
In engineering design, we usually target a Factor of Safety (FoS) between 1.5 and 3.0.
The center of the circle always represents the average normal stress at that point.
Always select a motor with a 15-20% safety margin over the calculated power.
In turbulent flow, doubling the velocity increases the pressure drop by a factor of four.
Always design columns with a high safety factor against buckling, as initial imperfections can trigger failure early.

It is a simplification of linear elastic theory which provides a means of calculating the load-carrying and deflection characteristics of beams.
From δ = PL³/(48EI) — span is the dominant sensitivity in serviceability.
A common floor deflection limit — total load deflection must not exceed span/360.
Short, thick beams where shear deformation is significant — Euler–Bernoulli underestimates δ.
L10 is life in millions of revolutions, while L10h is life in operating hours, which depends on the rotational speed (RPM).
Yes, poor lubrication significantly reduces bearing life by increasing friction and heat, potentially leading to premature surface fatigue.
A single radial load producing the same life as the actual combined radial and axial loading.
Contact stress-life relationship for point contact — rollers use p = 10/3 for line contact.
No — L10 is a statistical rating; field life depends on lubrication, contamination, and mounting.
It ensures that only one edge of the insert is doing the heavy work, reducing heat and preventing vibration (chatter).
Radial loads both edges equally; modified flank (3°–5°) loads one edge for stability and heat control.
Force per unit chip area — material property used to estimate Fc = ap × f × Kc.
Work hardening and springback require 2–3 more passes than carbon steel tables suggest.
The meter (m).
The inch was redefined in 1959 so that 1 in = 25.4 mm exactly — use this value, not 25.0, for precision fits.
No. kg is mass; N is force (kg·m/s²). Weight in newtons equals mass times local gravity.
Convert only at presentation boundaries — carry SI internally through all calculation steps.
A moment is a measure of the tendency of a force to cause a body to rotate about a specific point or axis.
An isolated sketch of a body showing all external forces and moments — the mandatory first step in any statics solution.
Yes. Both conditions are required; otherwise the body accelerates rotationally even if net force is zero.
Include self-weight when it is comparable to applied loads — especially for long beams and heavy fixtures.
Over-torquing a bolt causes it to exceed its yield strength, leading to plastic deformation, thread stripping, or sudden failure.
Lubrication drastically reduces the friction coefficient (K). A lubricated bolt requires significantly less torque to achieve the same preload compared to a dry bolt.
Torque is an indirect measure. The actual goal is to achieve clamping force (preload) to hold the joint together securely. Using preload calculation ensures accurate joint strength.
K ≈ 0.20 for unlubricated steel-on-steel; drops to ~0.12 with moly or oil.
No — scatter of ±25% is common. Torque + angle or ultrasonic measurement improves control.
Critical joints in automotive, pressure vessels, and aerospace where proof of clamp force is mandatory.
Threaded fasteners (bolts and screws) are by far the most widely used elements in engineering.
Machine elements are standardized, cataloged components with defined interfaces and performance ratings.
When permanent, high-stiffness joints are needed and disassembly is not required — but inspectability suffers.
Rolling-element bearings use balls/rollers for low friction; bushings are plain sleeves suited to oscillating or dirty environments.
Pressure is an external force distributed over an external surface, while stress is an internal resistance distributed over an internal cross-section.
Ratio of allowable to computed stress — typically 1.5–3.0 depending on consequence of failure and load uncertainty.
Shear acts parallel to the area; area is the section in the plane of shear, not perpendicular to force.
Beyond proportional limit / yield — plastic deformation requires different constitutive models.
It is a normal stress that acts on a plane where the shear stress is exactly zero.
Yes — σ1 and σ2 are invariants; only the orientation of principal planes changes with loading.
One principal stress is zero (thin plates, free surfaces) — simplifies 3D problems to 2D.
Triaxial stress states require 3D failure criteria (von Mises in full tensor form).
1 HP is approximately 0.746 kW.
A multiplier accounting for shock, reversing, and daily duty — applied to calculated power before motor selection.
Covers voltage variation, bearing drag increase over life, and production tolerances.
Motor torque = load torque / (ratio × efficiency) — higher ratio reduces motor torque demand.
It is a dimensionless quantity that helps predict flow patterns in fluid flow situations.
ρV²/2 — kinetic energy per unit volume; doubling velocity quadruples dynamic pressure.
Higher relative roughness ε/D increases friction factor, especially in turbulent regime.
Diameter sets velocity and ΔP — undersized pipe wastes pump energy; oversized adds cost.
6g is the standard commercial fit, while 4h is a tighter, precision fit used in high-vibration environments.
6g is external thread tolerance (bolt); 6H is internal (nut). Lower numbers mean tighter fits.
Historical British standard — incompatible with 60° metric/UN profiles despite similar appearance.
Power screws and lead screws where efficiency and load transmission matter more than fast assembly.
It is a quantity used to describe resistance to torsional deformation of a cylindrical object.
Multiplier on actual length: K=1 pinned-pinned, K=0.5 fixed-fixed, K=2 cantilever.
Yes — buckling is stability failure, not material overload.
Use equivalent torque and bending moment interaction — pure torsion analysis is insufficient.
δ: Deflection (m)
F: Point load at center (N)
L: Beam length (m)
E: Modulus of elasticity (Pa)
I: Area moment of inertia (m⁴)
L10: Basic rating life (millions of revolutions)
C: Basic dynamic load rating (N)
P: Equivalent dynamic bearing load (N)
p: Exponent for life equation (3 for ball, 10/3 for roller)
ap: Depth of Cut (mm)
f: Feed per Revolution (mm/rev)
Kc: Specific Cutting Force (N/mm²)
mm: Millimeters
inch: Inches (Imperial)
ΣF: Sum of all forces (N)
ΣM: Sum of all moments (Nm)
T: Torque (Nm)
K: Friction coefficient (0.18–0.25 typical)
F: Preload force (N)
d: Nominal bolt diameter (m)
Elements: Fasteners, Gears, Bearings, etc.
Power: Torque × Speed
σ: Normal Stress (Pa or MPa)
F: Axial Force (N)
A: Cross-sectional Area (m²)
σ1,2: Principal Stresses (MPa)
σx, σy: Normal stresses in X and Y (MPa)
τxy: Shear stress (MPa)
9550: Conversion constant
P: Power (kW)
n: Rotational speed (RPM)
ΔP: Pressure drop (Pa)
f: Darcy friction factor
L: Pipe length (m)
D: Pipe diameter (m)
ρ: Fluid density (kg/m³)
v: Fluid velocity (m/s)
Metrik (M): 60° Profile Angle
Unified (UN): 60° Profile Angle
Whitworth (W): 55° Profile Angle
Trapezoidal (Tr): 30° Profile Angle
Pcr: Critical Buckling Load (N)
E: Modulus of Elasticity (Pa)
I: Least Moment of Inertia (m⁴)
L: Unsupported length (m)
K: Column effective length factor
Convert between SI and Imperial length, mass, and force units without ambiguity
Identify when mass (kg) must not be substituted for force (N) in equilibrium equations
Apply ISO/DIN rounding rules for manufacturing tolerances expressed in microns
Always carry SI internally; convert only at presentation boundaries
1 in = 25.4 mm exactly — never approximate for precision fits
Unit errors propagate silently until they cause field failures
Every AluCalc kernel assumes SI internally. This lesson trains you to spot mixed-unit traps before they reach a drawing or BOM — the same discipline NASA mandates after the Mars Orbiter loss.
Draw complete free-body diagrams with correct force directions
Apply ΣF = 0 and ΣM = 0 to solve for unknown reactions
Decompose inclined forces into orthogonal components
A missing force on the FBD invalidates every downstream result
Choose the moment center with the most unknowns to simplify algebra
Statics is prerequisite to every structural and machine design check
Before you size a beam or a bolt, you must know the reactions at supports. The truss and beam engines in AluCalc assume you have already established equilibrium — this module teaches that foundation.
Classify machine elements by function: power transmission, support, and joining
Map design requirements to standard catalog components (ISO, DIN)
Recognize when a custom element is justified versus over-engineering
Standard elements enable interchangeability and lower lifecycle cost
Environment (corrosion, temperature, vibration) drives element selection
Design for disassembly unless the joint is intentionally permanent
Machine design is not inventing new parts — it is composing proven elements into a reliable system. This overview connects each element family to the specialized lessons and calculators that follow.
Distinguish 60° metric/UN threads from 55° Whitworth and 30° trapezoidal profiles
Read tolerance designations (6g/6H, 4h) and their assembly implications
Estimate stress area from pitch and major diameter for fastener sizing
Profile angle mismatch destroys threads on first assembly
Tolerance class controls fit — 4h is tighter than 6g for critical joints
Partial-profile tools trade flexibility for crest sharpness
Thread geometry is the DNA of every bolted joint. CNC programmers and fastener designers share the same ISO tables — this lesson aligns your vocabulary with the Thread Geometry engine.
Relate applied torque to bolt preload via friction coefficients (K or μ)
Apply VDI 2230 concepts: yield utilization and thread stripping checks
Select lubrication strategy and its effect on required tightening torque
Torque is an indirect measure of preload — friction dominates scatter
Over-torquing can yield the bolt and reduce clamp force after relaxation
Always verify both internal and external thread capacity
A torque wrench reading is not the design output — clamp force is. This lesson bridges shop-floor tightening to the VDI 2230 kernel used in AluCalc Fastener Lab.
Compute L10 life using ISO 281 dynamic rating and equivalent load P
Apply life exponent p = 3 for ball bearings and 10/3 for rollers
Interpret catalog C versus operating P and reliability targets
L10 means 10% of bearings fail before that life at 90% reliability
P > C is a critical overspeed/overload condition — resize immediately
Equivalent load combines radial and axial components per ISO rules
Bearing selection is a fatigue problem, not a static one. The ISO 281 engine in AluCalc uses the same C/P exponent you will apply in this lesson.
Convert torque and speed to shaft power using P = T·n/9550 (kW)
Account for transmission efficiency when sizing the motor nameplate
Distinguish continuous duty from peak starting torque requirements
Undersized motors overheat; oversized motors waste capital and efficiency
Always include gearbox losses in the motor power calculation
Add 15–20% margin for production variability and aging
Drive sizing connects mechanical load to electrical supply. The belt and motor calculators in AluCalc expect power in kW derived from the same torque–speed relationship taught here.
Compute normal stress σ = F/A and interpret tension vs compression
Apply Hooke's law to relate stress, strain, and elastic modulus
Identify stress concentrations at holes, fillets, and sudden section changes
Stress is internal resistance; pressure acts on external surfaces
Stay below yield with an appropriate factor of safety (1.5–3.0)
Shear area lies in the plane of the shear force — not the normal area
Statics gives external reactions; strength of materials tells you whether the material survives inside. Every stress calculator in AluCalc builds on σ = F/A and ε = σ/E.
Transform plane-stress components to find principal stresses σ1, σ2
Compute maximum shear τ_max from Mohr circle geometry
Explain why principal values are invariant while σ(θ) and τ(θ) rotate
Principal planes have zero shear — design checks often use σ1
τ_max = Mohr circle radius — drives yielding in ductile materials
Combined loading requires plane-stress analysis before failure criteria
Uniaxial σ = F/A is not enough when bending and torsion coexist. The Mohr Circle Lab visualizes the same transformation the OS kernel evaluates analytically.
Calculate shear stress in shafts under torsion using τ = Tr/J
Determine Euler critical load Pcr for slender columns
Select effective length factor K from end support conditions
Buckling fails on the weakest axis — always use minimum I
Slenderness ratio L/r determines whether Euler theory applies
Combined torsion and bending require interaction checks beyond this lesson
Sudden buckling collapse can occur below material yield stress. The column stability engine applies the same π²EI/(KL)² formula with your actual end conditions.
Apply Euler–Bernoulli deflection formulas for common support cases
Explain why deflection scales with L⁴ for center-loaded simply supported beams
Relate serviceability limits (e.g. L/360) to user comfort and gasket integrity
Deflection often governs design before stress reaches yield
Stiffness scales with E·I — doubling I halves deflection
Self-weight and creep may matter for long-span or polymer beams
Strength without serviceability is a failed design. Roark and AISC deflection limits are enforced in the Beam Deflection Engine using the same PL³/(48EI) backbone.
Apply Darcy–Weisbach to relate friction factor, geometry, and velocity head
Estimate Reynolds number to select laminar vs turbulent friction correlations
Size pumps using pressure drop and required flow rate
ΔP scales with f·(L/D)·ρV²/2 — velocity dominates in small pipes
Larger diameter cuts velocity quadratically for the same flow
Always verify NPSH after calculating line losses
Fluid systems are sized on flow and pressure, not just pipe schedule. The Darcy–Weisbach kernel in AluCalc uses the identical friction–geometry relationship derived here.
Select chip breaker geometry (TF, TQ) based on material and operation
Compare radial infeed vs modified flank infeed for threading stability
Relate depth of cut, feed, and specific cutting force to tool load
Uncontrolled chips cause bird-nesting — the primary threading failure mode
Modified flank feed reduces heat load on one insert edge
Minimum depth of cut is required to activate the chip breaker groove
Threading is a metal-cutting problem disguised as a geometry problem. Kyocera TQ data in this lesson feeds the same specific-force model used in the Machining Time engine.
